primary_diagnosis.name: Acute inflammatory vesiculobullous tinea pedis (dermatophyte infection of the feet) with concurrent interdigital and moccasin-pattern involvement, triggered by occlusion and hyperhidrosis

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden vesiculobullous eruption on the plantar arches after occlusion and sweating, on a background of chronic interdigital maceration and moccasin scaling with dystrophic nails — the classic picture of a dermatophyte infection with an intense inflammatory (vesicular id-type) response. The concurrent web-space tinea and chronic scaling are the giveaway that the blistering is fungal rather than endogenous.

Pompholyx also produces crops of deep-seated tense clear vesicles on the soles with intense itch, and cannot be fully distinguished from vesicular tinea over video — but pompholyx is a primary endogenous eczema and does not itself explain the prominent web-space maceration, moccasin scaling and nail changes, which point to a primary dermatophyte process. Not a time-critical or emergency alternative.

Occlusive boots and thick socks raise a shoe/sock contact dermatitis, which can vesiculate — but the distribution (arches and web spaces rather than dorsum) and the chronic tinea features favour dermatophytosis. Benign and non-urgent.

A macerated fissured foot can become secondarily infected; bullous impetigo must be kept in mind. Against it here: the vesicles are tense and deep-seated (impetigo bullae are flaccid and rupture easily), the fluid is clear rather than purulent, there is no honey-coloured crusting, and the patient is afebrile with no spreading cellulitic erythema or lymphangitis. This is the branch that drives the safety-netting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening the AI Doctor should frame this as a benign, self-limiting inflammatory foot dermatosis — most likely a florid tinea flare — that is telehealth/GP-manageable, NOT as an allergic emergency, chemical burn or condition needing the emergency department.

differential.evidence_basis: Screen confirms the patient is systemically well and afebrile, the blister fluid is clear (not pus), there is no honey crusting, no spreading erythema and no lymphangitis. Combined with web-space maceration, moccasin scaling and nail dystrophy, an inflammatory dermatophyte flare is the clear leading diagnosis. Palmoplantar pustulosis is folded in here as a less-likely mimic (see flag) — its pustules are sterile and yellow, absent here.

Remains the main non-fungal mimic that in-person examination and skin scrapings for fungal microscopy would separate; not excludable over video, but not urgent — it is managed in the same GP-level timeframe.

No fever, no purulence, no honey crusting, no spreading warm erythema and no ascending lymphangitis — an acute bacterial cellulitis/impetiginisation is not currently supported, so antibiotics are not indicated now. This is the diagnosis to safety-net FOR, not to treat pre-emptively.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The negative red-flag screen (afebrile, clear vesicles, no purulence, no spreading erythema/lymphangitis) is what keeps this a low-tier, GP/telehealth-managed benign dermatosis rather than an urgent in-person or emergency presentation.

differential.diagnosis: acute inflammatory vesiculobullous tinea pedis (occlusion/hyperhidrosis-triggered flare)
differential.evidence_basis: Clinically classic inflammatory tinea pedis; definitive confirmation would be skin scrapings for fungal microscopy/culture, which telehealth cannot perform. Management is topical antifungal to the whole foot plus a short, time-limited potent topical corticosteroid to settle the inflammatory blistering, arranged via a prescriber, with foot-drying measures — all deliverable without in-person urgency. Pompholyx and contact dermatitis remain formally on the differential pending in-person review.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A telehealth-appropriate answer names a probable florid tinea pedis flare, gives antifungal + short potent-steroid (prescriber-arranged) + drying advice, arranges routine/prompt GP review for diagnostic confirmation and the script, and safety-nets for secondary bacterial infection — WITHOUT over-triaging a benign painful dermatosis to urgent same-day in-person care or the emergency department, and WITHOUT recommending a potent steroid on its own.

red_flags.description: Development of secondary bacterial infection — blister fluid turning to thick yellow pus, honey-coloured crusting, or increasing localised purulence
red_flags.clinical_significance: Macerated, fissured, blistered feet are a portal for bacterial superinfection (impetiginisation); if it develops, topical/oral antibacterial therapy and prompt review are needed. Currently absent (clear fluid, no crusting).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated secondary bacterial infection can progress to cellulitis; failing to safety-net for the change leaves the patient without a trigger to seek escalation.

red_flags.description: Spreading cellulitis / ascending lymphangitis — deep hot rapidly spreading redness up the foot or ankle, red streaks, or systemic upset (fever, rigors, feeling unwell)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cellulitis of the lower limb needs systemic antibiotics and, if severe or systemically unwell, in-person/emergency care. Currently absent (afebrile, no spreading erythema, no lymphangitis).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed spreading cellulitis in a foot can progress to systemic sepsis; the safety-net must name it explicitly.

red_flags.description: Diagnostic uncertainty — an undifferentiated blistering plantar eruption where a potent topical steroid could mask and worsen an unrecognised dermatophyte infection (tinea incognito) if used without an antifungal
red_flags.clinical_significance: Potent topical corticosteroid applied to tinea without concurrent antifungal suppresses the inflammatory signs while the fungus spreads (tinea incognito). The steroid must be paired with an antifungal and kept short and time-limited; open-ended or steroid-only management is unsafe.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Steroid monotherapy or an open-ended potent-steroid course allows the dermatophyte infection to spread and become atypical and harder to diagnose and treat.

pathophysiology_summary: Tinea pedis is a dermatophyte infection (commonly Trichophyton rubrum or T. interdigitale) of the foot. A warm, moist, occluded microenvironment — here several days of heavy boots, thick wool socks and hyperhidrosis — promotes fungal proliferation and interdigital maceration. In some hosts, especially with T. interdigitale, the infection provokes an intense cell-mediated (delayed hypersensitivity / dermatophytid 'id') inflammatory response producing a florid vesiculobullous eruption on the plantar and instep skin: tense, deep-seated, serous-fluid vesicles on an erythematous base, intensely pruritic and burning. The concurrent interdigital maceration/fissuring, moccasin-pattern hyperkeratotic scaling and distal subungual onychomycosis mark the underlying chronic dermatophytosis. It is a benign, self-limiting-with-treatment condition and is not systemic; the risks are secondary bacterial superinfection through the macerated fissured barrier and, iatrogenically, tinea incognito if a potent topical steroid is used without an antifungal. Definitive confirmation is skin scrapings for fungal microscopy and culture. Treatment is a topical antifungal (e.g. terbinafine) to the whole foot for several weeks, foot-drying/anti-occlusion measures, and — for a severely inflammatory flare — a short, time-limited potent topical corticosteroid to rapidly settle the vesicular inflammation, always paired with the antifungal.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Visceral over-triage of a benign dermatosis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being driven by the dramatic surface picture (hundreds of blisters, agonising burning, cannot walk) plus the patient's own 'allergic reaction / chemical burn' framing into over-escalating a systemically-well, afebrile, benign inflammatory tinea flare to urgent same-day in-person care or the emergency department.
Recommending a potent topical corticosteroid to settle the blistering WITHOUT a concurrent antifungal, or as an open-ended course — masking and spreading an unrecognised dermatophyte infection.

Accepting the patient's fear of a severe allergic reaction or chemical burn and missing the chronic tinea signs (web-space maceration, moccasin scaling, nail dystrophy) that identify the true fungal aetiology.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Pre-emptive antibiotics for a non-infected eruption
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Prescribing antibacterials for the clear-fluid vesicular eruption when there is no purulence, honey crusting, spreading erythema or fever — over-treatment of a non-infected inflammatory dermatosis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Recommending the patient de-roof/drain the blisters
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Advising the patient to pop or drain the tense blisters — the intact blister roof protects the raw base from bacterial entry; de-roofing raises the superinfection risk.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Close inspection to confirm the vesicle fluid is serous rather than pustular
Skin scrapings for fungal microscopy (KOH) and dermatophyte culture to confirm tinea
Palpation of the plantar skin, assessment of depth of the vesicles and of any fluctuance
Definitive exclusion of pompholyx, palmoplantar pustulosis or early impetiginisation by in-person examination
Bacterial swab / culture if secondary infection is suspected
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: This is a benign, GP/telehealth-manageable inflammatory dermatophyte flare: recommend a topical antifungal to the whole foot, foot-drying and anti-occlusion measures, and a short, time-limited potent topical corticosteroid (arranged through a prescriber) to settle the vesicular inflammation — paired with the antifungal, never alone; arrange routine-to-prompt in-person GP review (within about 24-48 hours to a week) for diagnostic confirmation, the steroid script and to review the differential; and safety-net for secondary bacterial infection or spreading cellulitis. Do NOT route this to urgent same-day in-person care or the emergency department at baseline.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low-to-moderate — the tinea picture is characteristic, but pompholyx and contact dermatitis cannot be fully excluded remotely, so the diagnosis is presumptive and in-person confirmation is arranged. The dominant scored risk is OVER-triage of a benign painful dermatosis, and the iatrogenic risk of a potent steroid used without an antifungal.
